senses.gloss: to act immorally, to be wicked, to sin

examples.english: but man desires to act sinfully in what is before him

senses.gloss: to cleave, to break up, to dig up
to create an outlet or passage (for water)

examples.text: إِذَا السَّمَاء ٱنْفَطَرَتْ ۝ وَإِذَا ٱلْكَوَاكِبُ ٱنْتَثَرَتْ ۝ وَإِذَا ٱلْبِحَارُ فُجِّرَتْ ۝ وَإِذَا ٱلْقُبُورُ بُعْثِرَتْ ۝ عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ وَأَخَّرَتْ ۝
examples.english: When the sky breaks apart / And when the stars commence to scatter / And when the seas gush forth / And when the graves are strewn / A soul will know what it has put forward and what it has set back.

senses.gloss: verbal noun of فَجَرَ (fajara, “to break up, to sin”) (form I)

examples.text: فِي الْفَجْرِ الْبَاكِرِ، كَانَتِ الشُّوَارِعُ هَادِئَةً وَخَالِيَةً مِنَ الزُّحَامِ.
examples.english: In the early dawn, the streets were quiet and free from traffic.
Abdulala Maududi: All peace is that night until the rise of dawn.

senses.gloss: اَلْفَجْر (al-fajr): ellipsis of صَلَاة الْفَجْر (ṣalāt al-fajr, “dawn prayer”)

examples.english: we pray the dawn prayer in congregration

etymology.text: From Proto-Semitic *pagr- (“body, trunk”). Cognate with Akkadian 𒇿 (pagrum, “body, trunk, torso, corpse, carcass”), Hebrew פֶּגֶר (péger, “corpse, carcass”), Classical Syriac ܦܲܓܪܵܐ (paḡrā), and Classical Mandaic ࡐࡀࡂࡓࡀ (pāġrā).
